title: On the canonical, fpqc and finite topologies: classical questions, new answers (and conversely)
speaker: Yves André
affiliation: Institut de Mathématiques de Jussieu-Paris Rive Gauche
abstract: Up to a finite covering, a sequence of nested subvarieties of an affine algebraic variety just looks like a flag of vector spaces (Noether); understanding this « up to » is a primary motivation for a fine study of finite coverings.

proof_stages.label: Finite coverings and purity of ring maps
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates finite surjective morphisms from algebraic geometry and commutative algebra, then introduces purity of a ring map as universal injectivity and records basic splitting and faithful-flatness criteria, including Ferrand's question.

proof_stages.label: Pure morphisms as effective descent morphisms
proof_stages.summary: After recalling descent data for modules, the speaker states Olivier's theorem that the comparison functor for module descent is an equivalence exactly for pure maps, not merely for faithfully flat maps.

proof_stages.label: Canonical topology as the topology of purity on affines
proof_stages.summary: Grothendieck pretopologies and the canonical topology are recalled, and the affine canonical covers are identified with pure ring maps via an equalizer argument, placing Olivier descent in topological form.

proof_stages.label: Canonical finite covers and the direct summand theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker asks when finite covers are canonical, gives noncanonical phenomena for non-normal and normal bases, and then states that every finite cover of a regular scheme is canonical, equivalently the direct summand theorem for finite extensions of regular rings.

proof_stages.label: fpqc finite-cover theorem and canonical-but-not-fpqc covers
proof_stages.summary: The talk contrasts canonical and fpqc topologies by describing finite canonical covers that are not fpqc covers, answering Ferrand's question negatively, and then states the stronger theorem that every finite cover of a regular scheme is an fpqc-topology cover, equivalent locally to the existence of big Cohen-Macaulay algebras.

proof_stages.label: Perfectoid and prismatic construction of big Cohen-Macaulay algebras
proof_stages.summary: To justify the fpqc theorem, the speaker sketches the perfectoid construction by adjoining all p-power roots, using almost flatness and Gabber's trick to remove 'almost', and then recalls Bhatt's prismatic total-integral-closure construction that produces faithfully flat big algebras uniformly.

proof_stages.label: Frobenius, splinters, and singularities
proof_stages.summary: The finite-cover perspective is applied to Frobenius, F-purity, strong F-regularity, mixed-characteristic singularities, and splinters, leading to the conjectural fpqc analogue that only regular schemes have all finite covers fpqc-locally covering.

proof_stages.label: Descent along the fpqc topology as an open program
proof_stages.summary: The closing postscript proposes systematically revisiting EGA-style faithfully-flat descent results with fpqc-topology covers, where the covering morphism need not itself be flat but admits a further faithfully flat refinement.

examples.engagement_reason: The quotient of affine 4-space by a cyclic group in characteristic two is used to prove non-canonicity by applying Cohen–Macaulay descent and the known non-Cohen–Macaulay property.

examples.name: Characteristic-zero resolution as a derived Cohen-Macaulay object

examples.engagement_reason: The characteristic-zero resolution is analyzed via Grauert–Riemenschneider vanishing and local duality to derive vanishing of local cohomology, making it a derived Cohen–Macaulay object.
